papergraph.title: Quantum Generative Adversarial Networks For Anomaly Detection In High Energy Physics
papergraph.citation: Elie Bermot et al. (2023) Quantum Generative Adversarial Networks For Anomaly Detection In High Energy Physics doi:10.1109/QCE57702.2023.00045
papergraph.venue: International Conference on Quantum Computing and Engineering
papergraph.abstract: The standard model (SM) of particle physics represents a theoretical paradigm for the description of the fundamental forces of nature. Despite its broad applicability, the SM does not enable the description of all physically possible events. The detection of events that cannot be described by the SM, which are typically referred to as anomalous, and the related potential discovery of exotic physical phenomena is a non-trivial task. The challenge becomes even greater with next-generation colliders that will produce even more events with additional levels of complexity. The additional data complexity motivates the search for unsupervised anomaly detection methods that do not require prior knowledge about the underlying models. In this work, we develop such a technique. More explicitly, we employ a quantum generative adversarial network to identify anomalous events. The method learns the background distribution from SM data and, then, determines whether a given event is characteristic for the learned background distribution. The proposed quantum-powered anomaly detection strategy is tested on proof-of-principle examples using numerical simulations and IBM Quantum processors. We find that the quantum generative techniques using ten times fewer training data samples can yield comparable accuracy to the classical counterpart for the detection of the Graviton and Higgs particles. Additionally, we empirically compute the capacity of the quantum model and observe an improved expressivity compared to its classical counterpart.

papergraph.title: Fine-tuning machine-learned particle-flow reconstruction for new detector geometries in future colliders
papergraph.citation: Farouk Mokhtar et al. (2025) Fine-tuning machine-learned particle-flow reconstruction for new detector geometries in future colliders doi:10.1103/PhysRevD.111.092015
papergraph.abstract: We demonstrate transfer learning capabilities in a machine-learned algorithm trained for particle-flow reconstruction in high energy particle colliders. This paper presents a cross-detector fine-tuning study, where we initially pretrain the model on a large full simulation dataset from one detector design, and subsequently fine-tune the model on a sample with a different collider and detector design. Specifically, we use the Compact Linear Collider detector (CLICdet) model for the initial training set and demonstrate successful knowledge transfer to the CLIC-like detector (CLD) proposed for the Future Circular Collider in electron-positron mode. We show that with an order of magnitude less samples from the second dataset, we can achieve the same performance as a costly training from scratch, across particle-level and event-level performance metrics, including jet and missing transverse momentum resolution. Furthermore, we find that the fine-tuned model achieves comparable performance to the traditional rule-based particle-flow approach on event-level metrics after training on 100,000 CLD events, whereas a model trained from scratch requires at least 1 million CLD events to achieve similar reconstruction performance. To our knowledge, this represents the first full-simulation cross-detector transfer learning study for particle-flow reconstruction. These findings offer valuable insights towards building large foundation models that can be fine-tuned across different detector designs and geometries, helping to accelerate the development cycle for new detectors and opening the door to rapid detector design and optimization using machine learning.

papergraph.title: Foundation models for high-energy physics
papergraph.citation: Anna Hallin (2025) Foundation models for high-energy physics arXiv:2509.21434v2
papergraph.abstract: The rise of foundation models -- large, pretrained machine learning models that can be finetuned to a variety of tasks -- has revolutionized the fields of natural language processing and computer vision. In high-energy physics, the question of whether these models can be implemented directly in physics research, or even built from scratch, tailored for particle physics data, has generated an increasing amount of attention. This review, which is the first on the topic of foundation models in high-energy physics, summarizes and discusses the research that has been published in the field so far.

papergraph.title: Zero-Shot Image Anomaly Detection Using Generative Foundation Models
papergraph.citation: Lemar Abdi et al. (2025) Zero-Shot Image Anomaly Detection Using Generative Foundation Models arXiv:2507.22692v1
papergraph.abstract: Detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs is pivotal for deploying safe vision systems in open-world environments. We revisit diffusion models, not as generators, but as universal perceptual templates for OOD detection. This research explores the use of score-based generative models as foundational tools for semantic anomaly detection across unseen datasets. Specifically, we leverage the denoising trajectories of Denoising Diffusion Models (DDMs) as a rich source of texture and semantic information. By analyzing Stein score errors, amplified through the Structural Similarity Index Metric (SSIM), we introduce a novel method for identifying anomalous samples without requiring re-training on each target dataset. Our approach improves over state-of-the-art and relies on training a single model on one dataset -- CelebA -- which we find to be an effective base distribution, even outperforming more commonly used datasets like ImageNet in several settings. Experimental results show near-perfect performance on some benchmarks, with notable headroom on others, highlighting both the strength and future potential of generative foundation models in anomaly detection.
strengths: zero-shot capability without re-training per dataset
outperforms ImageNet-based baselines in several settings

keynotes.title: Quantum Generative Adversarial Networks For Anomaly Detection In High Energy Physics

keynote.title: Quantum Generative Adversarial Networks For Anomaly Detection In High Energy Physics
keynote.core_problem: Unsupervised detection of anomalous particle physics events—those not describable by the Standard Model—in increasingly complex collider data, without requiring prior knowledge of exotic models.
contributions: Proposes a quantum generative adversarial network (QGAN) for unsupervised anomaly detection in high-energy physics.
Demonstrates that the quantum method achieves comparable accuracy to classical GANs using ten times fewer training samples for Graviton and Higgs anomaly detection.
Empirically shows improved model expressivity (capacity) of the quantum variant relative to its classical counterpart.
methods: Learning background distribution from Standard Model (SM) simulation data.
Anomaly scoring via deviation from learned quantum-generated background distribution.
experiments_or_evidence: Physical execution on IBM Quantum processors.
Performance comparison against classical GANs on Graviton and Higgs anomaly detection tasks.
Empirical computation of model capacity to assess expressivity.
assumptions: Standard Model data sufficiently represents background (non-anomalous) event distribution.
Quantum hardware noise levels permit meaningful QGAN training and inference at scale used.
Anomalous events manifest as statistically significant deviations from SM background in feature space modeled by QGAN.
limitations: Evaluation limited to proof-of-principle examples; no large-scale collider data validation reported.
No discussion of scalability to high-dimensional event representations or real-time inference constraints.
Quantum hardware limitations (e.g., qubit count, coherence time, gate fidelity) likely constrain architectural depth and training stability—though not explicitly quantified.

gap_signals.text: Evaluation limited to proof-of-principle examples; no large-scale collider data validation reported.
No discussion of scalability to high-dimensional event representations or real-time inference constraints.
Quantum hardware limitations (e.g., qubit count, coherence time, gate fidelity) likely constrain architectural depth and training stability—though not explicitly quantified.
Classical unsupervised methods require large amounts of training data; quantum approach claims comparable accuracy with ten times fewer samples.
Empirical observation of improved expressivity in quantum model versus classical counterpart suggests representational limitations in classical architectures for this domain.
Next-generation colliders will produce more events with additional levels of complexity, motivating unsupervised anomaly detection methods that do not require prior knowledge about underlying models.

important_claims.claim: Quantum GANs achieve comparable accuracy to classical GANs for Graviton and Higgs anomaly detection using ten times fewer training samples.
important_claims.evidence: Reported finding from numerical simulations and IBM Quantum processor tests.

important_claims.claim: The quantum model exhibits improved expressivity compared to its classical counterpart.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a template for quantum-aided unsupervised discovery in domains with sparse anomalies and expensive simulation-based backgrounds; highlights data efficiency and expressivity as key quantum advantages worth isolating and benchmarking across other scientific ML tasks.

keynote.title: Zero-Shot Image Anomaly Detection Using Generative Foundation Models
keynote.core_problem: Detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs in open-world vision systems without retraining on target datasets.
contributions: Reframes diffusion models as universal perceptual templates—not generators—for zero-shot semantic anomaly detection.
Introduces a novel OOD detection method using Stein score errors amplified via SSIM on denoising trajectories of Denoising Diffusion Models (DDMs).

Demonstrates that a single model trained only on CelebA serves as an effective foundation for zero-shot anomaly detection across unseen datasets, outperforming ImageNet-based baselines in several settings.
methods: Analysis of denoising trajectories of Denoising Diffusion Models (DDMs).
Computation and amplification of Stein score errors using the Structural Similarity Index Metric (SSIM).
experiments_or_evidence: Comparison showing CelebA-trained model outperforms ImageNet-trained models in several settings.

important_claims.claim: Denoising trajectories of DDMs provide rich texture and semantic information useful for OOD detection.
important_claims.evidence: Stein score errors amplified via SSIM on these trajectories enable effective anomaly identification.

quantum generative adversarial network for unsupervised anomaly detection in high energy physics, achieving comparable accuracy with ten times fewer training samples and improved expressivity versus classical counterpart

Abstract: The standard model (SM) of particle physics represents a theoretical paradigm for the description of the fundamental forces of nature. Despite its broad applicability, the SM does not enable the description of all physically possible events. The detection of events that cannot be described...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:
The additional data complexity motivates the search for unsupervised anomaly detection methods that do not require prior knowledge about the underlying models.
The method learns the background distribution from SM data and, then, determines whether a given event is characteristic for the learned background distribution.

We find that the quantum generative techniques using ten times fewer training data samples can yield comparable accuracy to the classical counterpart for the detection of the Graviton and Higgs particles.
Additionally, we empirically compute the capacity of the quantum model and observe an improved expressivity compared to its classical counterpart.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: microscopy imaging
A zero-shot anomaly detection method using pre-trained diffusion models as perceptual templates via Stein score errors and SSIM-amplified denoising trajectories.
Notable headroom on others
Full text excerpt:

By analyzing Stein score errors, amplified through the Structural Similarity Index Metric (SSIM), we introduce a novel method for identifying anomalous samples without requiring re-training on each target dataset.

Our approach improves over state-of-the-art and relies on training a single model on one dataset -- CelebA -- which we find to be an effective base distribution, even outperforming more commonly used datasets like ImageNet in several settings.

Experimental results show near-perfect performance on some benchmarks, with notable headroom on others, highlighting both the strength and future potential of generative foundation models in anomaly detection.
